item.title: DEADet: Detail Enhancement with Multi-level Feature Joint Perception for Aircraft Fine-grained Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Fulin Luo
Yan Guan
Chuan Fu
Tan Guo
Yong Li
Bo Du
Liangpei Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Fine-grained aircraft detection in remote sensing images plays a crucial role in the field of military security, providing critical technical support for military reconnaissance, target tracking, and air defense early warning. However, the task suffers from both inadequate preservation of local features, which causes a loss of discriminative details, and a reliance on layer-specific feature interactions that fails to leverage cross-scale complementary information. To address these challenges, we propose a Detail Enhancement Aircraft Fine-grained Object Detection (DEADet), which is an efficient one-stage detector based on multi-level feature joint perception. First, to preserve fine-grained details, we design the Detail-Enhanced Feature Representation (DEFR) module based on a compress-enhance-reconstruct principle. Second, to achieve comprehensive cross-level feature perception and enhancement, we introduce the Multi-level Joint Feature Refiner (MJFR), which consists of a Multi-level Feature Fusion (MLFF) module and a Multi-level Feature Enhance (MLFE) module. Third, we propose the Normalized Angle and Center Loss (NAC Loss), incorporating both angle penalty and center penalty to improve orientation estimation and spatial localization, thereby guiding the model toward more precise feature learning. Extensive experiments on the FAIR1M-aircraft and MAR20 datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance with 88.87% and 85.51% mAP respectively, while maintaining high efficiency with only 23.07M parameters and 84.19G FLOPs, providing an effective solution for military remote sensing applications. The code is available at https://github.com/mysort/DEADet. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:46+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.813 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fulin Luo; Yan Guan; Chuan Fu; Tan Guo; Yong Li; Bo Du; Liangpei Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3703851

Relevance: 0.813 (must_read)

Fine-grained aircraft detection in remote sensing images plays a crucial role in the field of military security, providing critical technical support for military reconnaissance, target tracking, and air defense early warning. However, the task suffers from both inadequate preservation of local features, which causes a loss of discriminative details, and a reliance on layer-specific feature interactions that fails to leverage cross-scale complementary information. To address these challenges, we propose a Detail Enhancement Aircraft Fine-grained Object Detection (DEADet), which is an efficient one-stage detector based on multi-level feature joint perception. First, to preserve fine-grained details, we design the Detail-Enhanced Feature Representation (DEFR) module based on a compress-enhance-reconstruct principle. Second, to achieve comprehensive cross-level feature perception and enhancement, we introduce the Multi-level Joint Feature Refiner (MJFR), which consists of a Multi-level Feature Fusion (MLFF) module and a Multi-level Feature Enhance (MLFE) module. Third, we propose the Normalized Angle and Center Loss (NAC Loss), incorporating both angle penalty and center penalty to improve orientation estimation and spatial localization, thereby guiding the model toward more precise feature learning. Extensive experiments on the FAIR1M-aircraft and MAR20 datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance with 88.87% and 85.51% mAP respectively, while maintaining high efficiency with only 23.07M parameters and 84.19G FLOPs, providing an effective solution for military remote sensing applications. The code is available at https://github.com/mysort/DEADet.

item.title: LR-Depth: Enhancing Self-Supervised Monocular Depth Estimation in Low-Resolution Scenarios
item.dc:creator: Xiao Ke
Chaoying Chen
Jingyi Fang
Huanqi Wu
Yuzhen Niu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Existing self-supervised monocular depth estimation methods have achieved impressive results by reducing reliance on expensive labeled data. However, their performance degrades significantly in low-resolution scenes due to challenges like missing edge details, difficulty in modeling blur regions, and inaccuracies in upsampling geometric structure reconstruction. To address this issue, we propose LR-Depth, an efficient self-supervised monocular depth estimation framework tailored for low-resolution scenes. Firstly, we propose a multi-scale wavelet edge enhancement that captures high-frequency edge information to improve depth estimation in edge regions. Meanwhile, to improve blurred regions, we leverage wavelet blur mapping to refine low-frequency component representation and enhance the modeling accuracy of depth distributions. Finally, we introduce geometric reconstruction upsampling that integrates position initialization with local offset constraints, enabling precise recovery of complex geometric structures while ensuring geometric consistency of the depth map. Extensive experiments on the KITTI dataset demonstrate that LR-Depth achieves state-of-the-art performance in low-resolution self-supervised monocular depth estimation, particularly excelling in edge clarity and depth prediction for blurred regions. Additionally, generalizability experiments on the Make3D dataset further confirm the framework’s robustness and broad applicability. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:46+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiao Ke; Chaoying Chen; Jingyi Fang; Huanqi Wu; Yuzhen Niu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3703327

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

Existing self-supervised monocular depth estimation methods have achieved impressive results by reducing reliance on expensive labeled data. However, their performance degrades significantly in low-resolution scenes due to challenges like missing edge details, difficulty in modeling blur regions, and inaccuracies in upsampling geometric structure reconstruction. To address this issue, we propose LR-Depth, an efficient self-supervised monocular depth estimation framework tailored for low-resolution scenes. Firstly, we propose a multi-scale wavelet edge enhancement that captures high-frequency edge information to improve depth estimation in edge regions. Meanwhile, to improve blurred regions, we leverage wavelet blur mapping to refine low-frequency component representation and enhance the modeling accuracy of depth distributions. Finally, we introduce geometric reconstruction upsampling that integrates position initialization with local offset constraints, enabling precise recovery of complex geometric structures while ensuring geometric consistency of the depth map. Extensive experiments on the KITTI dataset demonstrate that LR-Depth achieves state-of-the-art performance in low-resolution self-supervised monocular depth estimation, particularly excelling in edge clarity and depth prediction for blurred regions. Additionally, generalizability experiments on the Make3D dataset further confirm the framework’s robustness and broad applicability.

item.title: LW-ResUNet: A Lightweight SAR Target Recognition Network Based on ResUNet
item.dc:creator: Haoye Chen
Yuying Zhu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Efficient target recognition in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery demands models with low computational cost. Developing lightweight SAR image recognition models has therefore become a significant research focus. This paper proposes LW-ResUNet, an improved lightweight network based on the ResUNet architecture, which maintains high recognition accuracy while drastically reducing parameters. The network introduces an asymmetric encoder-decoder that concentrates computational resources on feature extraction, a Parameter-Free Cross-Branch Connection (PF-CBC) that eliminates 1×1 convolutions via channel dimension matching, and a Self-Adaptive Channel Fusion (SCAF) module embedded in depthwise separable residual blocks for adaptive feature learning. To validate the method's effectiveness, we construct the FAIR-21 dataset. Experimental results show that with only 0.895M parameters, LW-ResUNet achieves 73.2% overall accuracy on FAIR-21 and 99.13% on MSTAR, outperforming mainstream lightweight models, outperforming mainstream lightweight models. The FAIR-21 dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/angelandeagle/LW-ResUnet-dataset. Published: 2026-06-15T19:50:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Haoye Chen; Yuying Zhu

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3703955

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Efficient target recognition in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery demands models with low computational cost. Developing lightweight SAR image recognition models has therefore become a significant research focus. This paper proposes LW-ResUNet, an improved lightweight network based on the ResUNet architecture, which maintains high recognition accuracy while drastically reducing parameters. The network introduces an asymmetric encoder-decoder that concentrates computational resources on feature extraction, a Parameter-Free Cross-Branch Connection (PF-CBC) that eliminates 1×1 convolutions via channel dimension matching, and a Self-Adaptive Channel Fusion (SCAF) module embedded in depthwise separable residual blocks for adaptive feature learning. To validate the method's effectiveness, we construct the FAIR-21 dataset. Experimental results show that with only 0.895M parameters, LW-ResUNet achieves 73.2% overall accuracy on FAIR-21 and 99.13% on MSTAR, outperforming mainstream lightweight models, outperforming mainstream lightweight models. The FAIR-21 dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/angelandeagle/LW-ResUnet-dataset.

item.title: Uncertainty-Aware Keypoint Guidance and Fractional Fourier Feature Enhancement for Multi-Class SAR Aircraft Detection
item.dc:creator: Yu Qiu
Bin Zou
Fangzhou Han
Lamei Zhang
Jordi J. Mallorqui
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: Aircraft targets in SAR imagery often exhibit discrete scattering characteristics, significant variations in pose and scale, strong speckle noise in background clutter, and complex background interference, which jointly hinder stable structural feature extraction and accurate target localization. Existing detectors for SAR aircraft recognition primarily rely on bounding-box regression and classification; they do not completely exploit target structural cues, spatial attention, and frequency-domain information. To address these limitations, we propose a collaborative detection framework that integrates an uncertainty-aware keypoint-driven module (UAKM) with a fractional Fourier convolution backbone (S-FRConv). UAKM introduces a center-keypoint regression branch that jointly predicts keypoint coordinates and Laplacian scale parameters and employs a 2D Laplace negative log-likelihood loss to estimate uncertainty. The derived dense uncertainty heatmap is then used as spatial attention weights to guide distribution-based regression and multi-scale feature re-weighting, without requiring any additional annotations. S-FRConv embeds the Fractional Fourier Transform into shallow backbone layers and C2f modules, enabling joint spatial–spectral feature modeling that suppresses speckle noise and enhances edge and orientation representations. Experiments on the public SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset demonstrate that the proposed method systematically improves the detection performance. For the Nano model, the overall mAP50 increases from 0.810 to 0.867, and the mAP 50:95 improves from 0.637 to 0.655 compared with the baseline, corresponding to gains of 5.7 and 1.8 percentage points, respectively. These results validate the effectiveness and generalization potential of combining uncertainty-driven spatial attention with fractional spectral feature enhancement for SAR aircraft target detection. Published: 2026-06-15T03:01:27+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.777 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yu Qiu; Bin Zou; Fangzhou Han; Lamei Zhang; Jordi J. Mallorqui

Journal: Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.3390/rs18121969

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Aircraft targets in SAR imagery often exhibit discrete scattering characteristics, significant variations in pose and scale, strong speckle noise in background clutter, and complex background interference, which jointly hinder stable structural feature extraction and accurate target localization. Existing detectors for SAR aircraft recognition primarily rely on bounding-box regression and classification; they do not completely exploit target structural cues, spatial attention, and frequency-domain information. To address these limitations, we propose a collaborative detection framework that integrates an uncertainty-aware keypoint-driven module (UAKM) with a fractional Fourier convolution backbone (S-FRConv). UAKM introduces a center-keypoint regression branch that jointly predicts keypoint coordinates and Laplacian scale parameters and employs a 2D Laplace negative log-likelihood loss to estimate uncertainty. The derived dense uncertainty heatmap is then used as spatial attention weights to guide distribution-based regression and multi-scale feature re-weighting, without requiring any additional annotations. S-FRConv embeds the Fractional Fourier Transform into shallow backbone layers and C2f modules, enabling joint spatial–spectral feature modeling that suppresses speckle noise and enhances edge and orientation representations. Experiments on the public SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset demonstrate that the proposed method systematically improves the detection performance. For the Nano model, the overall mAP50 increases from 0.810 to 0.867, and the mAP 50:95 improves from 0.637 to 0.655 compared with the baseline, corresponding to gains of 5.7 and 1.8 percentage points, respectively. These results validate the effectiveness and generalization potential of combining uncertainty-driven spatial attention with fractional spectral feature enhancement for SAR aircraft target detection.

item.title: MFDPrompt: Multi-Modal Fine-Grained Dual-Prompt Learning for Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection
item.dc:creator: Wenrui Hu
Tianyi Zhu
Fanxia Zeng
Yongqiang Tang
Rui Chen
Wensheng Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) has emerged as a promising paradigm for identifying anomalies without category-specific training. However, current vision-language model (VLM)-based ZSAD methods primarily focus on coarse-grained or fine-grained abnormality semantics, while neglecting two critical aspects: (1) the semantic duality between fine-grained normality and abnormality, and (2) the mining of intrinsic fine-grained abnormal and normal visual information within images to enhance semantic sample adaptability, which limits their discriminative power. To address these challenges, we propose MFDPrompt, a novel framework that explicitly models the semantic duality between normal and abnormal representations through Multi-modal Fine-grained Dual Prompts. MFDPrompt incorporates three innovative components: Fine-grained Dual Text Prompts (FDTP) for generating complementary text prompt pairs with semantic disentanglement, Fine-grained Dual Visual Prompts (FDVP) for dynamic adaptation to target datasets via visual query-tuning, and a Multi-modal Prompt Generator (MPG) for fusing text and visual prompts through two-way cross-attention. Extensive experiments across 19 industrial and medical benchmarks demonstrate that MFDPrompt achieves new state-of-the-art results in ZSAD, significantly outperforming the AnomalyCLIP baseline by 2.7-3.6% in image-level AUROC/AP metrics and 0.7-5.2% in pixel-level AUROC/PRO scores, with performance averaged across all datasets. Our work underscores the importance of jointly modeling both normal and abnormal semantics in fine-grained, multi-modal prompts, offering a robust and generalizable solution for ZSAD. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:46+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.774 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wenrui Hu; Tianyi Zhu; Fanxia Zeng; Yongqiang Tang; Rui Chen; Wensheng Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3703437

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

Zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) has emerged as a promising paradigm for identifying anomalies without category-specific training. However, current vision-language model (VLM)-based ZSAD methods primarily focus on coarse-grained or fine-grained abnormality semantics, while neglecting two critical aspects: (1) the semantic duality between fine-grained normality and abnormality, and (2) the mining of intrinsic fine-grained abnormal and normal visual information within images to enhance semantic sample adaptability, which limits their discriminative power. To address these challenges, we propose MFDPrompt, a novel framework that explicitly models the semantic duality between normal and abnormal representations through Multi-modal Fine-grained Dual Prompts. MFDPrompt incorporates three innovative components: Fine-grained Dual Text Prompts (FDTP) for generating complementary text prompt pairs with semantic disentanglement, Fine-grained Dual Visual Prompts (FDVP) for dynamic adaptation to target datasets via visual query-tuning, and a Multi-modal Prompt Generator (MPG) for fusing text and visual prompts through two-way cross-attention. Extensive experiments across 19 industrial and medical benchmarks demonstrate that MFDPrompt achieves new state-of-the-art results in ZSAD, significantly outperforming the AnomalyCLIP baseline by 2.7-3.6% in image-level AUROC/AP metrics and 0.7-5.2% in pixel-level AUROC/PRO scores, with performance averaged across all datasets. Our work underscores the importance of jointly modeling both normal and abnormal semantics in fine-grained, multi-modal prompts, offering a robust and generalizable solution for ZSAD.

item.title: Diffusion Disambiguation Models for Partial Label Learning
item.dc:creator: Jinfu Fan
Xiaohui Zhong
Kangrui Ren
Jiangnan Li
Linqing Huang
Min Gan
C. L. Philip Chen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Learning from ambiguous labels is a long-standing problem in practical machine learning applications. The purpose of partial label learning(PLL) is to identify the ground-truth label from a set of candidate labels associated with a given instance. Inspired by the remarkable performance of diffusion models in various generation tasks, this paper explores their potential to denoise ambiguous labels through the reverse denoising process. Therefore, this paper reformulates the label disambiguation problem from the perspective of generative models, where labels are generated by iteratively refining initial random guesses. This perspective enables the diffusion model to learn how label information is generated stochastically. By modeling the generation uncertainty, we can use the maximum likelihood estimate of the label for classification inference. However, such ambiguous labels lead to a mismatch between instance and label, which reduces the quality of generated data. To address this issue, this paper proposes a diffusion disambiguation model for PLL (DDMP), which first uses the potential complementary information between instances and labels to construct pseudo-clean labels for initial diffusion training. Furthermore, a transition-aware matrix is introduced to estimate the potential ground-truth labels, which are dynamically updated during the diffusion generation. During training, the ground-truth label is progressively refined, improving the classifier. Experiments show the advantage of the DDMP and its suitability for PLL. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:46+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.771 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jinfu Fan; Xiaohui Zhong; Kangrui Ren; Jiangnan Li; Linqing Huang; Min Gan; C. L. Philip Chen

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3703405

Relevance: 0.771 (must_read)

Learning from ambiguous labels is a long-standing problem in practical machine learning applications. The purpose of partial label learning(PLL) is to identify the ground-truth label from a set of candidate labels associated with a given instance. Inspired by the remarkable performance of diffusion models in various generation tasks, this paper explores their potential to denoise ambiguous labels through the reverse denoising process. Therefore, this paper reformulates the label disambiguation problem from the perspective of generative models, where labels are generated by iteratively refining initial random guesses. This perspective enables the diffusion model to learn how label information is generated stochastically. By modeling the generation uncertainty, we can use the maximum likelihood estimate of the label for classification inference. However, such ambiguous labels lead to a mismatch between instance and label, which reduces the quality of generated data. To address this issue, this paper proposes a diffusion disambiguation model for PLL (DDMP), which first uses the potential complementary information between instances and labels to construct pseudo-clean labels for initial diffusion training. Furthermore, a transition-aware matrix is introduced to estimate the potential ground-truth labels, which are dynamically updated during the diffusion generation. During training, the ground-truth label is progressively refined, improving the classifier. Experiments show the advantage of the DDMP and its suitability for PLL.

item.title: Unlocking In-Context Learning for Natural Datasets Across Modalities
item.dc:creator: Jelena Bratulić
Sudhanshu Mittal
David T. Hoffmann
Samuel Böhm
Robin Tibor Schirrmeister
Tonio Ball
Christian Rupprecht
Thomas Brox
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Computer Vision
item.description: Abstract Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit In-Context Learning (ICL), which enables the model to perform new tasks conditioning only on the examples provided in the context without updating the model’s weights. While ICL offers fast adaptation across natural language tasks and domains, its emergence is less straightforward for modalities beyond text. In this work, we systematically uncover properties present in LLMs that support the emergence of ICL for autoregressive models and various modalities by promoting the learning of the mechanisms needed for ICL. We identify exact token repetitions in the training data sequences as an important factor for ICL. Such repetitions further improve stability and reduce transiency in ICL performance. We analyse in detail the training dynamics of such data sequences and explain how token repetitions enhance the ICL learning mechanisms. Moreover, we emphasise the importance of the training task difficulty for the emergence of ICL. Finally, by applying our novel insights on ICL emergence, we unlock ICL capabilities across various visual datasets used for few-shot classification, and confirm the generalisability of our insights to much harder real-world examples of large-scale object classification, and a more challenging EEG classification task. Code is available at https://github.com/jelenab98/unlocking_icl Published: 2026-06-15T08:10:15+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.762 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jelena Bratulić; Sudhanshu Mittal; David T. Hoffmann; Samuel Böhm; Robin Tibor Schirrmeister; Tonio Ball; Christian Rupprecht; Thomas Brox

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02913-0

Relevance: 0.762 (consider)

Abstract Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit In-Context Learning (ICL), which enables the model to perform new tasks conditioning only on the examples provided in the context without updating the model’s weights. While ICL offers fast adaptation across natural language tasks and domains, its emergence is less straightforward for modalities beyond text. In this work, we systematically uncover properties present in LLMs that support the emergence of ICL for autoregressive models and various modalities by promoting the learning of the mechanisms needed for ICL. We identify exact token repetitions in the training data sequences as an important factor for ICL. Such repetitions further improve stability and reduce transiency in ICL performance. We analyse in detail the training dynamics of such data sequences and explain how token repetitions enhance the ICL learning mechanisms. Moreover, we emphasise the importance of the training task difficulty for the emergence of ICL. Finally, by applying our novel insights on ICL emergence, we unlock ICL capabilities across various visual datasets used for few-shot classification, and confirm the generalisability of our insights to much harder real-world examples of large-scale object classification, and a more challenging EEG classification task. Code is available at https://github.com/jelenab98/unlocking_icl

item.title: BDRNet: Background-Aware Dynamic-Scale Routing Network for UAV Remote Sensing Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Xuelong Zheng
Faming Shao
Qing Liu
Juying Dai
Yiming Yue
Tao Zhang
Caian Chen
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: Object detection in UAV remote sensing imagery remains challenging due to severe scale variation, dense object distributions, complex background clutter, and localization ambiguity caused by extremely small objects. To address these issues, this paper proposes BDRNet, a lightweight background-aware dynamic-scale routing network for UAV remote sensing object detection. First, a background-aware feature enhancement (BAFE) module is introduced into the backbone to enhance feature representation through horizontal and vertical contextual modeling, improving target-related responses in complex aerial scenes. Second, a dynamic-scale routing pyramid (DSRP) is designed to retain the high-resolution P2 branch and adaptively integrate multi-scale features through spatially dynamic routing, alleviating the loss of fine-grained information and improving the representation of small and scale-varied objects. Third, a scale- and geometry-aware normalized Wasserstein distance (SGNW) loss is proposed by modeling bounding boxes as two-dimensional Gaussian distributions. By incorporating aspect-ratio-guided geometric weighting and scale-aware dynamic fusion, SGNW improves regression stability for small objects while preserving geometric constraints for medium and large targets. Extensive experiments on the VisDrone2019 and UAVDT datasets demonstrate that BDRNet consistently improves detection accuracy over the YOLOv10s detector while maintaining a comparable model size and computational cost. Compared with several mainstream lightweight detectors, BDRNet achieves a favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off, demonstrating its effectiveness for UAV remote sensing object detection in complex aerial scenarios. Published: 2026-06-15T15:00:48+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.757 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xuelong Zheng; Faming Shao; Qing Liu; Juying Dai; Yiming Yue; Tao Zhang; Caian Chen

Journal: Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.3390/rs18121987

Relevance: 0.757 (consider)

Object detection in UAV remote sensing imagery remains challenging due to severe scale variation, dense object distributions, complex background clutter, and localization ambiguity caused by extremely small objects. To address these issues, this paper proposes BDRNet, a lightweight background-aware dynamic-scale routing network for UAV remote sensing object detection. First, a background-aware feature enhancement (BAFE) module is introduced into the backbone to enhance feature representation through horizontal and vertical contextual modeling, improving target-related responses in complex aerial scenes. Second, a dynamic-scale routing pyramid (DSRP) is designed to retain the high-resolution P2 branch and adaptively integrate multi-scale features through spatially dynamic routing, alleviating the loss of fine-grained information and improving the representation of small and scale-varied objects. Third, a scale- and geometry-aware normalized Wasserstein distance (SGNW) loss is proposed by modeling bounding boxes as two-dimensional Gaussian distributions. By incorporating aspect-ratio-guided geometric weighting and scale-aware dynamic fusion, SGNW improves regression stability for small objects while preserving geometric constraints for medium and large targets. Extensive experiments on the VisDrone2019 and UAVDT datasets demonstrate that BDRNet consistently improves detection accuracy over the YOLOv10s detector while maintaining a comparable model size and computational cost. Compared with several mainstream lightweight detectors, BDRNet achieves a favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off, demonstrating its effectiveness for UAV remote sensing object detection in complex aerial scenarios.

item.title: U3A: Uncertainty-Aware Accident Anticipation for Reliable Autonomous Driving
item.dc:creator: Xingcheng Liu
Bin Rao
Yanchen Guan
Wanping Liu
Chengyue Wang
Xuesong Wang
Dengbo He
Yang Liu
Zhenning Li
Keqiang Li
Chengzhong Xu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Accident anticipation is essential for autonomous driving, yet existing video-based models often exhibit unstable predictions because they insufficiently account for the uncertainty that accumulates over long temporal sequences. Such instability leads to fluctuating confidence and unreliable alert timing in complex traffic environments. We propose U3A, an uncertainty-aware framework that improves the reliability of accident anticipation by jointly stabilizing representations and regulating decision behavior. U3A introduces a dual diffusion refinement module that enforces temporal coherence at both image and object levels, reducing abrupt feature shifts caused by occlusion and scene variability. To quantify epistemic uncertainty, the framework applies Monte Carlo Dropout over historical states and incorporates these estimates into a time-sensitive actor–critic policy, which learns to delay alerts under unstable confidence and issue warnings only when risk is consistently supported. Experiments on DAD, CCD, A3D, and AoTA show that U3A achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and mean time-to-accident, while producing smoother and more dependable alert trajectories. The results demonstrate that integrating diffusion-based feature refinement with uncertainty-guided policy learning offers a practical path toward more reliable accident anticipation for autonomous vehicles. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.755 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xingcheng Liu; Bin Rao; Yanchen Guan; Wanping Liu; Chengyue Wang; Xuesong Wang; Dengbo He; Yang Liu; Zhenning Li; Keqiang Li; Chengzhong Xu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tits.2026.3696318

Relevance: 0.755 (consider)

Accident anticipation is essential for autonomous driving, yet existing video-based models often exhibit unstable predictions because they insufficiently account for the uncertainty that accumulates over long temporal sequences. Such instability leads to fluctuating confidence and unreliable alert timing in complex traffic environments. We propose U3A, an uncertainty-aware framework that improves the reliability of accident anticipation by jointly stabilizing representations and regulating decision behavior. U3A introduces a dual diffusion refinement module that enforces temporal coherence at both image and object levels, reducing abrupt feature shifts caused by occlusion and scene variability. To quantify epistemic uncertainty, the framework applies Monte Carlo Dropout over historical states and incorporates these estimates into a time-sensitive actor–critic policy, which learns to delay alerts under unstable confidence and issue warnings only when risk is consistently supported. Experiments on DAD, CCD, A3D, and AoTA show that U3A achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and mean time-to-accident, while producing smoother and more dependable alert trajectories. The results demonstrate that integrating diffusion-based feature refinement with uncertainty-guided policy learning offers a practical path toward more reliable accident anticipation for autonomous vehicles.

item.title: Robust Lane Change Decision for Autonomous Vehicles: A Causal-Invariant Adversarial Reinforcement Learning Approach
item.dc:creator: Yongjie Wang
Kaiyue Wang
Yuchen Niu
Zixuan Zhang
Qiong Li
Xinhua Mao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Despite the rapid development of autonomous driving technology, the lane-changing polices of autonomous vehicles still face two major challenges: perceptual uncertainty and vulnerability to adversarial attacks. Existing approaches often rely on empirical regularization or adversarial training to handle input perturbations, but they lack a systematic modeling of the causal relationship between perturbation mechanisms and policy deviations, making it difficult to fundamentally improve robustness. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel causal-invariant adversarial reinforcement learning (CIARL) approach to enhance the robustness and safety of autonomous vehicles under optimal observational perturbations. The approach primarily comprises two core modules: the attacker employs a closed-box perturbation generator based on Bayesian optimization to construct optimal adversarial perturbations in the feature representation mapping space; the defender incorporates a causal-invariant representation learning mechanism to mitigate attacks by reducing the influence of non-causal features. This mechanism constrains the impact of adversarial perturbations on policy and cost within a controllable range while maximizing rewards, thereby enabling robust policy learning. Finally, the proposed CIARL approach is evaluated in the SUMO simulator across complex highway and on-ramp scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines in both lane-change robustness and collision safety, verifying the core role of the causal-invariant mechanism in adversarial robust policy modeling. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.754 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yongjie Wang; Kaiyue Wang; Yuchen Niu; Zixuan Zhang; Qiong Li; Xinhua Mao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tits.2026.3698480

Relevance: 0.754 (consider)

Despite the rapid development of autonomous driving technology, the lane-changing polices of autonomous vehicles still face two major challenges: perceptual uncertainty and vulnerability to adversarial attacks. Existing approaches often rely on empirical regularization or adversarial training to handle input perturbations, but they lack a systematic modeling of the causal relationship between perturbation mechanisms and policy deviations, making it difficult to fundamentally improve robustness. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel causal-invariant adversarial reinforcement learning (CIARL) approach to enhance the robustness and safety of autonomous vehicles under optimal observational perturbations. The approach primarily comprises two core modules: the attacker employs a closed-box perturbation generator based on Bayesian optimization to construct optimal adversarial perturbations in the feature representation mapping space; the defender incorporates a causal-invariant representation learning mechanism to mitigate attacks by reducing the influence of non-causal features. This mechanism constrains the impact of adversarial perturbations on policy and cost within a controllable range while maximizing rewards, thereby enabling robust policy learning. Finally, the proposed CIARL approach is evaluated in the SUMO simulator across complex highway and on-ramp scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines in both lane-change robustness and collision safety, verifying the core role of the causal-invariant mechanism in adversarial robust policy modeling.

item.title: LMFusion: Breaking the Computational Barrier for Multimodal Classification in Remote Sensing
item.dc:creator: Shenbo Zhou
Sibo He
Daixun Li
Weiying Xie
Yunsong Li
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: Multi-modal land cover classification plays an important role in remote sensing applications such as urban monitoring and environmental analysis. By integrating complementary information from hyperspectral imagery (HSI) and LiDAR data, multimodal learning can significantly improve classification performance. However, existing Transformer-based fusion methods often suffer from high computational complexity and inefficient cross-modal interaction modeling, which limits their applicability in resource-constrained scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose LMFusion, an efficient framework for multimodal feature learning. Specifically, LMFusion enables efficient bidirectional feature interaction through a linear-complexity cross-attention mechanism and enhances long-range spatial-spectral representation learning with Mamba-based state space modeling, thereby achieving effective multimodal dependency modeling with linear computational complexity. In addition, a selective quantization-aware optimization strategy is introduced to support multiple bit-width settings (down to 1-bit), yielding a more compact and efficient model while improving representation robustness under low-bit constraints. Extensive experiments on the Houston2013, MUUFL, and Augsburg datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of LMFusion. It achieves overall accuracies of 95.84%, 94.95%, and 99.05%, respectively, consistently outperforming representative multimodal classification methods and showing strong potential for accurate and efficient multimodal remote sensing classification. Published: 2026-06-15T03:01:27+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.752 (consider)

Multi-modal land cover classification plays an important role in remote sensing applications such as urban monitoring and environmental analysis. By integrating complementary information from hyperspectral imagery (HSI) and LiDAR data, multimodal learning can significantly improve classification performance. However, existing Transformer-based fusion methods often suffer from high computational complexity and inefficient cross-modal interaction modeling, which limits their applicability in resource-constrained scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose LMFusion, an efficient framework for multimodal feature learning. Specifically, LMFusion enables efficient bidirectional feature interaction through a linear-complexity cross-attention mechanism and enhances long-range spatial-spectral representation learning with Mamba-based state space modeling, thereby achieving effective multimodal dependency modeling with linear computational complexity. In addition, a selective quantization-aware optimization strategy is introduced to support multiple bit-width settings (down to 1-bit), yielding a more compact and efficient model while improving representation robustness under low-bit constraints. Extensive experiments on the Houston2013, MUUFL, and Augsburg datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of LMFusion. It achieves overall accuracies of 95.84%, 94.95%, and 99.05%, respectively, consistently outperforming representative multimodal classification methods and showing strong potential for accurate and efficient multimodal remote sensing classification.

item.title: Learning 3D Scene Reconstruction From Nighttime Driving Videos
item.dc:creator: Andrea Ramazzina
Stefano Gasperini
Mario Bijelic
Felix Heide
Federico Tombari
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
item.description: Neural Radiance Fields and Gaussian Splatting have emerged as powerful representations for reconstructing complex dynamic urban scenes from multi-view driving logs. By producing photorealistic and geometrically consistent renderings, these methods offer a foundation for closed-loop simulation as well as scalable data augmentation engine, enabling the synthesis of diverse viewpoints and conditions without the need for costly additional data collection in the real world. However, existing approaches are almost exclusively tailored to well-lit, daytime environments and struggle with the challenges of nighttime settings, where noise, strong flares, and moving light sources dominate the visual signal. To address these limitations, we propose NightSplat, a Gaussian Splatting framework specifically designed for high-fidelity reconstruction and novel view synthesis of nighttime driving scenes. Through dedicated components, our method explicitly models sensor noise, lens flare effects, and introduces a lightweight module to represent dynamic vehicle lights. Extensive evaluations on nuScenes and Waymo Open Dataset demonstrate that NightSplat significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art baselines, both quantitatively and qualitatively, thereby extending simulation and testing to nighttime scenes. Project page: night-splat.github.io Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:11+00:00 Venue: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters Score: 0.749 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Andrea Ramazzina; Stefano Gasperini; Mario Bijelic; Felix Heide; Federico Tombari

Journal: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lra.2026.3703261

Relevance: 0.749 (consider)

Neural Radiance Fields and Gaussian Splatting have emerged as powerful representations for reconstructing complex dynamic urban scenes from multi-view driving logs. By producing photorealistic and geometrically consistent renderings, these methods offer a foundation for closed-loop simulation as well as scalable data augmentation engine, enabling the synthesis of diverse viewpoints and conditions without the need for costly additional data collection in the real world. However, existing approaches are almost exclusively tailored to well-lit, daytime environments and struggle with the challenges of nighttime settings, where noise, strong flares, and moving light sources dominate the visual signal. To address these limitations, we propose NightSplat, a Gaussian Splatting framework specifically designed for high-fidelity reconstruction and novel view synthesis of nighttime driving scenes. Through dedicated components, our method explicitly models sensor noise, lens flare effects, and introduces a lightweight module to represent dynamic vehicle lights. Extensive evaluations on nuScenes and Waymo Open Dataset demonstrate that NightSplat significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art baselines, both quantitatively and qualitatively, thereby extending simulation and testing to nighttime scenes. Project page: night-splat.github.io

item.title: W-Diffusion: A Probabilistic Generative Framework for Traffic Forecasting via Shallow Diffusion Mechanism
item.dc:creator: Zhepu Yang
Shuangxun Ma
Tao Dai
Peixin Chen
Ruixuan Zhang
Daniel Jian Sun
Xiangmo Zhao
Shirui Pan
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Timely-accurate traffic forecasting is crucial for urban traffic guidance and control. Accurate forecasting of traffic flow and speed are essential for analyzing road usage, alleviating congestion, and enabling real-time monitoring and dynamic navigation. These capabilities contribute to enhanced road safety and more efficient infrastructure operation. Most existing methods neglect the inherent uncertainties within the traffic scenario, which reduces their practicality for real-world traffic control. The newly emerged denoising diffusion probabilistic models bring new inspiration to address this issue. However, these models only involve a single generation process, which is not appropriate for traffic forecasting task. Furthermore, the impact of Gaussian noise in generative process brings more challenges for modeling signal correlations. To overcome these limitations, we present the first attempt to introduce the shallow diffusion mechanism into traffic forecasting task, leading to a novel framework called W-Diffusion. The proposed model consists of separated shallow and deep diffusion stages, in order to better capture the signal correlations during the generative process. We also devise a new denoising network AST-Unet, to combine the spatiotemporal learning capabilities of spatiotemporal graph neural networks with the uncertainty measurements of diffusion models. Comprehensive experimental evaluations are conducted on the Caltrans Performance Measurement System (PeMS) benchmarks to validate the effectiveness of the proposed model. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.746 (consider)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tits.2026.3695665

Relevance: 0.746 (consider)

Timely-accurate traffic forecasting is crucial for urban traffic guidance and control. Accurate forecasting of traffic flow and speed are essential for analyzing road usage, alleviating congestion, and enabling real-time monitoring and dynamic navigation. These capabilities contribute to enhanced road safety and more efficient infrastructure operation. Most existing methods neglect the inherent uncertainties within the traffic scenario, which reduces their practicality for real-world traffic control. The newly emerged denoising diffusion probabilistic models bring new inspiration to address this issue. However, these models only involve a single generation process, which is not appropriate for traffic forecasting task. Furthermore, the impact of Gaussian noise in generative process brings more challenges for modeling signal correlations. To overcome these limitations, we present the first attempt to introduce the shallow diffusion mechanism into traffic forecasting task, leading to a novel framework called W-Diffusion. The proposed model consists of separated shallow and deep diffusion stages, in order to better capture the signal correlations during the generative process. We also devise a new denoising network AST-Unet, to combine the spatiotemporal learning capabilities of spatiotemporal graph neural networks with the uncertainty measurements of diffusion models. Comprehensive experimental evaluations are conducted on the Caltrans Performance Measurement System (PeMS) benchmarks to validate the effectiveness of the proposed model.

item.title: The Fusion Augmentation Method for Underwater Acoustic Target Recognition
item.dc:creator: Ming Chen
Feng Wang
Yang ze Dong
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: In the field of underwater acoustic target recognition, scarce acoustic samples can cause models to overfit. To address this issue, we employ a combination of traditional data augmentation methods, the feature-based SpecAugment method, and the model-based conditional deep convolutional generative adversarial network (cDCGAN) data augmentation method to mitigate overfitting. For the feature-based SpecAugment method, we replace frequency deletion with filter-based bandwidth reduction. This approach not only preserves frequency information in the spectrogram but also better simulates the acoustic environment. For the model-based cDCGAN method, we draw inspiration from ensemble learning to enhance the quality of generated data and improve training stability. Finally, the augmented datasets are fed into an enhanced MobileViT network for recognition. Experimental results demonstrate that this method achieves recognition rates of 96.63% and 95.22% on the ShipsEar and DeepShip datasets respectively, outperforming both classical and state-of-the-art models. This approach provides valuable insights for applications such as speech recognition, audio processing, and high-precision sonar detection. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:53+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.745 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ming Chen; Feng Wang; Yang ze Dong

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3703382

Relevance: 0.745 (consider)

In the field of underwater acoustic target recognition, scarce acoustic samples can cause models to overfit. To address this issue, we employ a combination of traditional data augmentation methods, the feature-based SpecAugment method, and the model-based conditional deep convolutional generative adversarial network (cDCGAN) data augmentation method to mitigate overfitting. For the feature-based SpecAugment method, we replace frequency deletion with filter-based bandwidth reduction. This approach not only preserves frequency information in the spectrogram but also better simulates the acoustic environment. For the model-based cDCGAN method, we draw inspiration from ensemble learning to enhance the quality of generated data and improve training stability. Finally, the augmented datasets are fed into an enhanced MobileViT network for recognition. Experimental results demonstrate that this method achieves recognition rates of 96.63% and 95.22% on the ShipsEar and DeepShip datasets respectively, outperforming both classical and state-of-the-art models. This approach provides valuable insights for applications such as speech recognition, audio processing, and high-precision sonar detection.

item.title: Achieving Socially-Aware Car-Following in Mixed Traffic: A Safe Multi-Task Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
item.dc:creator: Xiao Wen
Jingyuan Zhou
Sisi Jian
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Current research on autonomous vehicle (AV) car-following control primarily emphasizes optimizing AV performance without considering interactions between AVs and human-driven vehicles (HDVs). This oversight can lead to socially non-compliant AV behaviors that confuse following HDVs, destabilize traffic flow, and increase congestion and crash risks. Moreover, while most studies employ safe deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods focused solely on AV safety, we argue that AVs, which are equipped with superior sensing and decision-making capabilities, should proactively contribute to the safety of following HDVs. To address these limitations, we propose a socially aware AV car-following controller based on social value orientation (SVO). Given the broad spectrum of SVO, we design a safe multi-task DRL (SMTDRL) framework that includes a single-task DRL backbone, a curriculum-based multi-task learning strategy for efficient cross-SVO knowledge transfer, and a differentiable safety module using control barrier functions (CBFs) to ensure safety for both the AV and its HDV followers. Comprehensive simulations demonstrate that SMTDRL improves traffic flow stability, ride comfort, and fuel economy while enforcing strict safety constraints at the platoon level, outperforming both single-task DRL and baseline multi-task DRL approaches. For example, in safety-critical scenarios, SMTDRL achieves a 100% collision-free rate, significantly outperforming baseline multi-task DRL approaches. In terms of traffic performance, it reduces the average cumulative damping ratio by approximately 7% compared to single-task DRL models. Furthermore, SMTDRL exhibits strong generalizability to unseen SVO values, highlighting its robust adaptability in mixed traffic environments. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.744 (consider)

This oversight can lead to socially non-compliant AV behaviors that confuse following HDVs, destabilize traffic flow, and increase congestion and crash risks. Moreover, while most studies employ safe deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods focused solely on AV safety, we argue that AVs, which are equipped with superior sensing and decision-making capabilities, should proactively contribute to the safety of following HDVs. To address these limitations, we propose a socially aware AV car-following controller based on social value orientation (SVO). Given the broad spectrum of SVO, we design a safe multi-task DRL (SMTDRL) framework that includes a single-task DRL backbone, a curriculum-based multi-task learning strategy for efficient cross-SVO knowledge transfer, and a differentiable safety module using control barrier functions (CBFs) to ensure safety for both the AV and its HDV followers. Comprehensive simulations demonstrate that SMTDRL improves traffic flow stability, ride comfort, and fuel economy while enforcing strict safety constraints at the platoon level, outperforming both single-task DRL and baseline multi-task DRL approaches. For example, in safety-critical scenarios, SMTDRL achieves a 100% collision-free rate, significantly outperforming baseline multi-task DRL approaches. In terms of traffic performance, it reduces the average cumulative damping ratio by approximately 7% compared to single-task DRL models. Furthermore, SMTDRL exhibits strong generalizability to unseen SVO values, highlighting its robust adaptability in mixed traffic environments.

item.title: S2R: From Screening to Recognition, Event-Driven Perception of Railway Defects in Low-Power Systems
item.dc:creator: Mingxu Li
Donghai Zhai
Shichun Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Automated, real-time inspection of railway infrastructure is critical for ensuring the safety of modern transportation systems. Deploying such perception systems on edge devices, however, is constrained by stringent power limitations. While Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are well-suited for low-power sensing, their non-differentiable spiking dynamics complicate direct training. In contrast, Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) achieve easier optimization convergence but incur sustained high-power computational requirements, making them ill-suited for continuous and off-grid monitoring. Thus, integrating a shallow SNN for simple yet high-frequency tasks with a deep ANN for more complex tasks emerges as a promising solution. In this work, we propose an event-driven perception framework for railway surface defect segmentation to enable real-time railway defect inspection in edge deployments. The SNN serves as a region proposal module to detect potential defects, and only the defective segments are forwarded to the ANN for finer segmentation. This design enables our model to achieve high performance on sparse target inspection tasks with minimal energy consumption. We also collected and annotated a dataset named DeTian Railway Surface Defect (DTRSD), comprising 1,126 real-world railway defect images captured under diverse natural lighting conditions. These images capture anomalies, such as dust deposits and rust stains, offering a realistic environment for evaluating segmentation methods. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves competitive accuracy while reducing energy consumption relative to existing approaches. The code and dataset will be publicly available at https://github.com/lidadadaxu/HybridRSD Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.742 (consider)

Automated, real-time inspection of railway infrastructure is critical for ensuring the safety of modern transportation systems. Deploying such perception systems on edge devices, however, is constrained by stringent power limitations. While Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are well-suited for low-power sensing, their non-differentiable spiking dynamics complicate direct training. In contrast, Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) achieve easier optimization convergence but incur sustained high-power computational requirements, making them ill-suited for continuous and off-grid monitoring. Thus, integrating a shallow SNN for simple yet high-frequency tasks with a deep ANN for more complex tasks emerges as a promising solution. In this work, we propose an event-driven perception framework for railway surface defect segmentation to enable real-time railway defect inspection in edge deployments. The SNN serves as a region proposal module to detect potential defects, and only the defective segments are forwarded to the ANN for finer segmentation. This design enables our model to achieve high performance on sparse target inspection tasks with minimal energy consumption. We also collected and annotated a dataset named DeTian Railway Surface Defect (DTRSD), comprising 1,126 real-world railway defect images captured under diverse natural lighting conditions. These images capture anomalies, such as dust deposits and rust stains, offering a realistic environment for evaluating segmentation methods. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves competitive accuracy while reducing energy consumption relative to existing approaches. The code and dataset will be publicly available at https://github.com/lidadadaxu/HybridRSD

item.title: Monocular Spatial Pose Estimation of the Overhead Contact System Dropper Clamp Based on Deep Learning
item.dc:creator: Jiuru Lu
Yu Cai
Mai Zhang
Zhirui He
Chuxiong Hu
Ze Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: In automated maintenance of high-speed rail, estimating the spatial pose of the overhead contact system (OCS) dropper clamp is crucial. This estimation is essential for robot operations like disassembly and assembly. However, the complex outdoor environment has drastic lighting variations, background clutter, and frequent occlusions, which undermines the stability and robustness of detection systems. Moreover, traditional pose estimation algorithms have low real-time performance. This limits maintenance efficiency and hinders rapid response and continuous operation in automated systems. To address various interference issues in outdoor scenarios, this study enhances the model’s adaptability to complex outdoor backgrounds by replacing the backgrounds of catenary clamp images to generate datasets under different weather conditions, and applying data augmentation techniques, such as flipping, luminance adjustment, and noise injection, to improve adaptability to various environments. A dataset of 31,104 annotated images is constructed. The dataset includes eleven geometrically distinct keypoints. Finally, the pose of the dropper clamp relative to the camera is computed using the EPnP (Efficient Perspective-n-Point) algorithm. Experiments show that the proposed monocular method is efficient, stable, and accurate. It is suitable for real-time automated maintenance in high-speed rail environments. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.740 (consider)

In automated maintenance of high-speed rail, estimating the spatial pose of the overhead contact system (OCS) dropper clamp is crucial. This estimation is essential for robot operations like disassembly and assembly. However, the complex outdoor environment has drastic lighting variations, background clutter, and frequent occlusions, which undermines the stability and robustness of detection systems. Moreover, traditional pose estimation algorithms have low real-time performance. This limits maintenance efficiency and hinders rapid response and continuous operation in automated systems. To address various interference issues in outdoor scenarios, this study enhances the model’s adaptability to complex outdoor backgrounds by replacing the backgrounds of catenary clamp images to generate datasets under different weather conditions, and applying data augmentation techniques, such as flipping, luminance adjustment, and noise injection, to improve adaptability to various environments. A dataset of 31,104 annotated images is constructed. The dataset includes eleven geometrically distinct keypoints. Finally, the pose of the dropper clamp relative to the camera is computed using the EPnP (Efficient Perspective-n-Point) algorithm. Experiments show that the proposed monocular method is efficient, stable, and accurate. It is suitable for real-time automated maintenance in high-speed rail environments.

item.title: Enhanced Vessel Trajectory Prediction With Novel Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System-Based Alpha–Beta Filter Using AIS Data
item.dc:creator: Junaid Khan
Umar Zaman
Ahmad ul Hassan
Sajjad Hussain
Eunkyu Lee
Kyungsup Kim
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: Shipping is integral to global trade, with increasing maritime traffic elevating safety risks. Accurate trajectory prediction based on Automatic Identification System (AIS) data is essential to prevent collisions and enhance navigational safety. This study introduces a novel Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System (MFIS)-based alpha-beta filter for vessel trajectory prediction, addressing the limitations of conventional static parameter tuning. The proposed method dynamically adjusts alpha and beta parameters using fuzzy logic and processes speed, heading, latitude, and longitude inputs to predict trajectories over extended periods. The fuzzy alpha-beta filter’s performance was evaluated on AIS data from 50 ships using metrics such as Average Displacement Error (ADE), Final Displacement Error (FDE), Non-Linear ADE (NL-ADE), and R 2 R^{2} . It achieved superior accuracy (ADE: 0.020, FDE: 0.031, NL-ADE: 0.011, R 2 R^{2} : 0.97) compared to conventional and deep learning models, demonstrating its efficacy in complex maritime scenarios. These findings mark a significant advancement in trajectory prediction systems, enhancing maritime safety and operational efficiency. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.740 (consider)

These findings mark a significant advancement in trajectory prediction systems, enhancing maritime safety and operational efficiency.

item.title: Enhancing thermal image-based intrusion detection with spatial-channel attention and multi-scale fusion
item.dc:creator: Adnan Khalil
Fakhre Alam
Dilawar Shah
Irshad Khalil
Sami Ur Rahman
Shujaat Ali
Muhammad Tahir
item.prism:publicationName: Neural Computing and Applications
item.description: Thermal imaging is increasingly applied for reliable intrusion detection in intelligent surveillance systems under dark environment. Yet, precise intruder posture classification (e.g., crawling, climbing) in thermal images is still a challenging task due to the low resolution, background noise, and weak spatial-spectral correlations. To the best of our knowledge, most existing methods neglect to consider the scale variations, and the inter-label dependencies, which decreases the capacity when applied to real-world scenarios. We introduce a Dual Attention Network (DAN) for multi-label thermal image classification, tailored for intrusion detection. The proposed methodology consists of a multi-scale feature extraction with ResNet-50 as the backbone to adapt with posture size variation, a Spatial Attention Module (SAM) to emphasize on posture related area, and a Channel Attention Module (CAM) to attend to inter-channel relations. The fusion layer then integrates enhance spatial and channel features to improve final predication. The proposed model was trained and evaluated on a synthetic thermal dataset (PDIWS), consisting of 2,000 training and 500 testing images representing five distant poses: creeping, crawling, stooping, climbing, and other. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieved the best results with 97.98% classification accuracy, surpassing the baselines CNNs (ResNet-50: 97.02%) and other backbone networks. Ablation experiments verified the integration of spatial attention (+ 0.33% accuracy) and channel attention (+ 0.39% accuracy). In order to improve the effectiveness, we used Grad-CAM to visualize the attention maps to confirm the model’s ability. Published: 2026-06-15T01:30:04+00:00 Venue: Neural Computing and Applications Score: 0.737 (consider)

Yet, precise intruder posture classification (e.g., crawling, climbing) in thermal images is still a challenging task due to the low resolution, background noise, and weak spatial-spectral correlations. To the best of our knowledge, most existing methods neglect to consider the scale variations, and the inter-label dependencies, which decreases the capacity when applied to real-world scenarios. We introduce a Dual Attention Network (DAN) for multi-label thermal image classification, tailored for intrusion detection. The proposed methodology consists of a multi-scale feature extraction with ResNet-50 as the backbone to adapt with posture size variation, a Spatial Attention Module (SAM) to emphasize on posture related area, and a Channel Attention Module (CAM) to attend to inter-channel relations. The fusion layer then integrates enhance spatial and channel features to improve final predication. The proposed model was trained and evaluated on a synthetic thermal dataset (PDIWS), consisting of 2,000 training and 500 testing images representing five distant poses: creeping, crawling, stooping, climbing, and other. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieved the best results with 97.98% classification accuracy, surpassing the baselines CNNs (ResNet-50: 97.02%) and other backbone networks. Ablation experiments verified the integration of spatial attention (+ 0.33% accuracy) and channel attention (+ 0.39% accuracy). In order to improve the effectiveness, we used Grad-CAM to visualize the attention maps to confirm the model’s ability.

item.title: An Improved YOLOv8n Method for Small Thermal Defect Detection of Photovoltaic Modules in UAV Infrared Inspection
item.dc:creator: Tengfei He
Zhongyuan Mao
Yuanchang Zhong
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: To address missed detections, false alarms, and deployment limitations in thermal defect detection of photovoltaic modules from unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) infrared images, this paper proposes an improved detection method based on You Only Look Once version 8 nano (YOLOv8n). The proposed method is optimized according to the characteristics of UAV infrared photovoltaic inspection, including small thermal targets, weak and diffuse thermal responses, complex backgrounds, and lightweight deployment requirements. Specifically, a P2 shallow feature layer is introduced to enhance fine-grained feature perception for small thermal defects, while Ghost Convolution (GhostConv) is incorporated into the backbone to reduce model complexity. In addition, C2f-Large Separable Kernel Attention (C2f-LSKA) is embedded in the neck to strengthen contextual and spatial feature modeling under complex infrared backgrounds, and Wise-IoU version 3 (WIoUv3) is adopted to improve bounding box regression and localization stability for boundary-ambiguous thermal anomalies. Experiments are conducted on a self-constructed UAV infrared thermal imaging dataset. From nearly 10,000 inspection images, 3000 representative images are selected and manually annotated, covering typical challenges such as small hot spots, low-contrast defects, complex background interference, and diffuse abnormal temperature-rise regions. Compared with the baseline YOLOv8n, the proposed method improves Precision, Recall, mean average precision at an IoU threshold of 0.5 (mAP@0.5), and mean average precision averaged over IoU thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95 (mAP@0.5:0.95) by 5.1, 11.4, 9.6, and 13.2 percentage points, respectively, while reducing the number of parameters and model size by 65.8% and 61.9%, respectively. These results indicate that the proposed method improves detection accuracy and localization quality under the evaluated UAV infrared inspection setting while maintaining lightweight characteristics. Published: 2026-06-15T08:57:58+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.736 (consider)

To address missed detections, false alarms, and deployment limitations in thermal defect detection of photovoltaic modules from unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) infrared images, this paper proposes an improved detection method based on You Only Look Once version 8 nano (YOLOv8n). The proposed method is optimized according to the characteristics of UAV infrared photovoltaic inspection, including small thermal targets, weak and diffuse thermal responses, complex backgrounds, and lightweight deployment requirements. Specifically, a P2 shallow feature layer is introduced to enhance fine-grained feature perception for small thermal defects, while Ghost Convolution (GhostConv) is incorporated into the backbone to reduce model complexity. In addition, C2f-Large Separable Kernel Attention (C2f-LSKA) is embedded in the neck to strengthen contextual and spatial feature modeling under complex infrared backgrounds, and Wise-IoU version 3 (WIoUv3) is adopted to improve bounding box regression and localization stability for boundary-ambiguous thermal anomalies. Experiments are conducted on a self-constructed UAV infrared thermal imaging dataset. From nearly 10,000 inspection images, 3000 representative images are selected and manually annotated, covering typical challenges such as small hot spots, low-contrast defects, complex background interference, and diffuse abnormal temperature-rise regions. Compared with the baseline YOLOv8n, the proposed method improves Precision, Recall, mean average precision at an IoU threshold of 0.5 (mAP@0.5), and mean average precision averaged over IoU thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95 (mAP@0.5:0.95) by 5.1, 11.4, 9.6, and 13.2 percentage points, respectively, while reducing the number of parameters and model size by 65.8% and 61.9%, respectively. These results indicate that the proposed method improves detection accuracy and localization quality under the evaluated UAV infrared inspection setting while maintaining lightweight characteristics.

item.title: Multi-Scale Dynamic Receptive Field for High-Resolution Satellite Stereo Matching
item.dc:creator: Wei Qin
Xin Huang
Yifan Liao
Lai Wei
Yang Yu
Wenxiang Gan
Qi Chen
Pengjie Tao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: High-resolution satellite stereo matching is fundamental for 3D mapping. While current deep learning models achieve impressive macroscopic geometric accuracy, they inherently rely on convolutions with static receptive fields. This rigidity struggles with extreme scale variations across complex landscapes, often causing pervasive localized matching errors, such as edge-fattening artifacts at object boundaries. Furthermore, applying standard dynamic convolutions to address this within massive 4D cost volumes incurs prohibitive memory overhead. To address this trade-off, we propose a parameter-efficient, scale-adaptive cost aggregation strategy. We introduce a Multi-scale Dynamic Receptive Field (MDRF) mechanism that decouples the learned base operator from the dilation-dependent sampling support. By reusing a single shared kernel across multiple dilations via a content-aware attention gate, the network adaptively weights multi-scale context responses. This plug-and-play module is parameter-efficient, with memory and throughput explicitly reported in the evaluated configurations. Evaluated on the WHU-Stereo and US3D benchmarks, our approach consistently lowers the outlier rate, achieving absolute D1-error reductions of 0.43 and 0.49 percentage points, respectively, compared with the baseline. Error-map analysis shows that MDRF reduces localized boundary artifacts, supporting a measured accuracy-efficiency trade-off for practical remote sensing applications. Published: 2026-06-15T19:50:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.734 (consider)

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
