Relevance: 0.734 (consider)

High-resolution satellite stereo matching is fundamental for 3D mapping. While current deep learning models achieve impressive macroscopic geometric accuracy, they inherently rely on convolutions with static receptive fields. This rigidity struggles with extreme scale variations across complex landscapes, often causing pervasive localized matching errors, such as edge-fattening artifacts at object boundaries. Furthermore, applying standard dynamic convolutions to address this within massive 4D cost volumes incurs prohibitive memory overhead. To address this trade-off, we propose a parameter-efficient, scale-adaptive cost aggregation strategy. We introduce a Multi-scale Dynamic Receptive Field (MDRF) mechanism that decouples the learned base operator from the dilation-dependent sampling support. By reusing a single shared kernel across multiple dilations via a content-aware attention gate, the network adaptively weights multi-scale context responses. This plug-and-play module is parameter-efficient, with memory and throughput explicitly reported in the evaluated configurations. Evaluated on the WHU-Stereo and US3D benchmarks, our approach consistently lowers the outlier rate, achieving absolute D1-error reductions of 0.43 and 0.49 percentage points, respectively, compared with the baseline. Error-map analysis shows that MDRF reduces localized boundary artifacts, supporting a measured accuracy-efficiency trade-off for practical remote sensing applications.

item.title: Coordinate-Aware Enhancement and Adaptive Hierarchical Decoding for Remote Sensing Semantic Change Detection
item.dc:creator: Qunlei Xu
Zhengyang Zhang
Hongfei Cao
Baojian Ren
Di Zhang
Xinglin Zhang
Tao Cao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Current hierarchical decoding frameworks for semantic change detection often suffer from boundary blurring due to direction-agnostic attention mechanisms and rigid feature concatenation. To address these limitations, we propose the Coordinate-Aware Adaptive Hierarchical Network (CA-AHNet). Specifically, we introduce a Coordinate-Aware Difference Intensifier Block (CA-DIB) to replace energy-based attention, enabling the precise capture of long-range directional dependencies crucial for linear targets. Furthermore, an Adaptive Weighted Hierarchical Feature Fusion Module (AW-HFFM) is designed to dynamically adjust the contribution of multi-scale features via learnable weights, effectively bridging the semantic gap between encoder layers. Experiments on the SECOND and Landsat-SCD datasets demonstrate that CA-AHNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior accuracy in both semantic segmentation and change detection tasks. Published: 2026-06-15T19:50:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.732 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qunlei Xu; Zhengyang Zhang; Hongfei Cao; Baojian Ren; Di Zhang; Xinglin Zhang; Tao Cao

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3703949

Relevance: 0.732 (consider)

Current hierarchical decoding frameworks for semantic change detection often suffer from boundary blurring due to direction-agnostic attention mechanisms and rigid feature concatenation. To address these limitations, we propose the Coordinate-Aware Adaptive Hierarchical Network (CA-AHNet). Specifically, we introduce a Coordinate-Aware Difference Intensifier Block (CA-DIB) to replace energy-based attention, enabling the precise capture of long-range directional dependencies crucial for linear targets. Furthermore, an Adaptive Weighted Hierarchical Feature Fusion Module (AW-HFFM) is designed to dynamically adjust the contribution of multi-scale features via learnable weights, effectively bridging the semantic gap between encoder layers. Experiments on the SECOND and Landsat-SCD datasets demonstrate that CA-AHNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior accuracy in both semantic segmentation and change detection tasks.

item.title: A Plot-to-Track Association Framework Based on Graph Representation Learning for Compact HFSWR
item.dc:creator: Xiaotong Li
Weifeng Sun
Yonggang Ji
Weimin Huang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: To address plot-to-track association ambiguities caused by low spatial resolution and measurement uncertainties of compact high-frequency surface wave radar (HFSWR) operating in complex maritime environments, a hybrid framework integrating a target state prediction method based on Transformer model and a plot-to-track association method using graph-based feature representation is proposed. In the target state prediction stage, the Transformer model employs self-attention mechanisms to capture long-term temporal dependencies in historical track data to improve the prediction accuracy of the plot-to-track association gate center. In the plot-to-track association stage, a graph autoencoder (GAE) is used to extract graph structure features of candidate measured plots from their corresponding range-Doppler (R-D) spectra. The GAE encodes the diffusion patterns and spatial correlations of radar echoes on the R-D spectrum into discriminative graph feature vectors. The graph feature vectors, which can enhance both inter-target discrimination and target-clutter differentiation, serve as the basis for association similarity computation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly reduces target state prediction errors and suppresses false plot-to-track associations, achieving an average tracking time on target of 135.6 minutes, which is improved by 36% compared with the baseline method (motion model-based target state prediction combined with nearest neighbor data association using kinematic parameters). Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:53+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.732 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaotong Li; Weifeng Sun; Yonggang Ji; Weimin Huang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3703385

Relevance: 0.732 (consider)

To address plot-to-track association ambiguities caused by low spatial resolution and measurement uncertainties of compact high-frequency surface wave radar (HFSWR) operating in complex maritime environments, a hybrid framework integrating a target state prediction method based on Transformer model and a plot-to-track association method using graph-based feature representation is proposed. In the target state prediction stage, the Transformer model employs self-attention mechanisms to capture long-term temporal dependencies in historical track data to improve the prediction accuracy of the plot-to-track association gate center. In the plot-to-track association stage, a graph autoencoder (GAE) is used to extract graph structure features of candidate measured plots from their corresponding range-Doppler (R-D) spectra. The GAE encodes the diffusion patterns and spatial correlations of radar echoes on the R-D spectrum into discriminative graph feature vectors. The graph feature vectors, which can enhance both inter-target discrimination and target-clutter differentiation, serve as the basis for association similarity computation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly reduces target state prediction errors and suppresses false plot-to-track associations, achieving an average tracking time on target of 135.6 minutes, which is improved by 36% compared with the baseline method (motion model-based target state prediction combined with nearest neighbor data association using kinematic parameters).

item.title: SA-TSDTP: A State-Aware Two-Stage Framework for Logistics Drone Trajectory Prediction
item.dc:creator: Chuanqi Ma
Lingshu Zhong
Huasa Zhu
Mingyang Pei
Ming Cai
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
item.description: The high flexibility and uncertainty of the flight trajectory are important factors affecting the operational safety of logistics drones. Reliable flight trajectory prediction is the core technology to solve this problem. In order to solve the multivariate time series prediction task of short-time flight trajectory prediction, this work proposes a state-aware two-stage prediction model. In the first stage, logistics drone trajectories are segmented into three flight states based on geometric characteristics and altitude change rates. In the second stage, a multi-expert fusion module incorporating flight state information is introduced to capture the underlying motion patterns of drones and enhance prediction accuracy. The proposed prediction model is evaluated using trajectory data collected from multiple real logistics drones in Shenzhen, China. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach exhibits strong potential and consistently outperforms baseline methods across multiple datasets and evaluation metrics. These findings provide valuable technical support for the future development of safe urban air traffic operations and smart cities. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:02+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems Score: 0.730 (consider)

Authors: Chuanqi Ma; Lingshu Zhong; Huasa Zhu; Mingyang Pei; Ming Cai

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tits.2026.3698652

Relevance: 0.730 (consider)

The high flexibility and uncertainty of the flight trajectory are important factors affecting the operational safety of logistics drones. Reliable flight trajectory prediction is the core technology to solve this problem. In order to solve the multivariate time series prediction task of short-time flight trajectory prediction, this work proposes a state-aware two-stage prediction model. In the first stage, logistics drone trajectories are segmented into three flight states based on geometric characteristics and altitude change rates. In the second stage, a multi-expert fusion module incorporating flight state information is introduced to capture the underlying motion patterns of drones and enhance prediction accuracy. The proposed prediction model is evaluated using trajectory data collected from multiple real logistics drones in Shenzhen, China. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach exhibits strong potential and consistently outperforms baseline methods across multiple datasets and evaluation metrics. These findings provide valuable technical support for the future development of safe urban air traffic operations and smart cities.

item.title: DPL: Depth-only Perceptive Humanoid Locomotion via Realistic Depth Synthesis and Cross-Attention Terrain Reconstruction
item.dc:creator: Jingkai Sun
Gang Han
Pihai Sun
Wen Zhao
Jiahang Cao
Jiaxu Wang
Qiang Zhang
Yijie Guo
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
item.description: Recent advancements in legged robot perceptive locomotion have shown promising progress. However, terrain-aware humanoid locomotion remains largely constrained to two paradigms: depth image-based end-to-end learning and elevation map-based methods. The former suffers from limited training efficiency and a significant sim-to-real gap in depth perception, while the latter depends heavily on multiple vision sensors and localization systems, resulting in latency and reduced robustness. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel framework that tightly integrates three key components: (1) Terrain-Aware Locomotion Policy with a Blind Backbone, which leverages pre-trained elevation map-based perception to guide reinforcement learning with minimal visual input; (2) Multi-Modality Cross-Attention Transformer, which reconstructs structured terrain representations from noisy depth images; (3) Realistic Depth Images Synthetic Method, which employs self-occlusion-aware ray casting and noise-aware modeling to synthesize realistic depth observations, achieving over 30% reduction in terrain reconstruction error. This combination enables efficient policy training with limited data and hardware resources, while preserving critical terrain features essential for generalization. We validate our framework on a full-sized humanoid robot, demonstrating agile and adaptive locomotion across diverse and challenging terrains. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:11+00:00 Venue: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters Score: 0.730 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jingkai Sun; Gang Han; Pihai Sun; Wen Zhao; Jiahang Cao; Jiaxu Wang; Qiang Zhang; Yijie Guo

Journal: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lra.2026.3703584

Relevance: 0.730 (consider)

Recent advancements in legged robot perceptive locomotion have shown promising progress. However, terrain-aware humanoid locomotion remains largely constrained to two paradigms: depth image-based end-to-end learning and elevation map-based methods. The former suffers from limited training efficiency and a significant sim-to-real gap in depth perception, while the latter depends heavily on multiple vision sensors and localization systems, resulting in latency and reduced robustness. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel framework that tightly integrates three key components: (1) Terrain-Aware Locomotion Policy with a Blind Backbone, which leverages pre-trained elevation map-based perception to guide reinforcement learning with minimal visual input; (2) Multi-Modality Cross-Attention Transformer, which reconstructs structured terrain representations from noisy depth images; (3) Realistic Depth Images Synthetic Method, which employs self-occlusion-aware ray casting and noise-aware modeling to synthesize realistic depth observations, achieving over 30% reduction in terrain reconstruction error. This combination enables efficient policy training with limited data and hardware resources, while preserving critical terrain features essential for generalization. We validate our framework on a full-sized humanoid robot, demonstrating agile and adaptive locomotion across diverse and challenging terrains.

item.title: A comprehensive review of U-Net architectures for medical image segmentation: emerging trends and federated learning perspectives
item.dc:creator: Md. Fahim Hossen
Anirban Sarkar
Turja Majumder
Md. Fahmidun Nabi
Md. Faysal Ahamed
Fariya Bintay Shafi
Rusab Sarmun
Muhammad E. H. Chowdhury
item.prism:publicationName: Neural Computing and Applications
item.description: Abstract U-Net has become a fundamental method in medical image segmentation with its architecture evolving to tackle complex segmentation tasks across modalities such as Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), Computed Tomography (CT) scans and microscopic images. This review offers a comprehensive analysis of various U-Net architectures including U-Net++, Residual U-Net, Dense U-Net and more recent transformer-based models like TransUNet and Swin-UNet. These architectures introduce optimizations like improved feature propagation, gradient flow and self-attention mechanisms, significantly enhancing segmentation accuracy. Despite its widespread success, U-Net faces limitations in handling data imbalance, computational complexity and challenges with multi-modal and large-scale data. The integration of federated learning with U-Net addresses privacy concerns by enabling secure, collaborative model training across healthcare institutions while maintaining data confidentiality. This review also highlights the applications of U-Net variants in key medical imaging tasks, including brain segmentation, retinal vessel segmentation, cell and nuclei segmentation, prostate segmentation and skin lesion segmentation, which play a critical role in early disease detection and treatment planning. In addition to exploring these applications, the paper examines benchmark datasets, loss functions and evaluation metrics essential for assessing U-Net architectures. The review also discusses emerging trends like self-supervised learning, lightweight models for resource-constrained environments and interpretability techniques, offering potential directions for future research. By presenting both the advancements and limitations of U-Net, this review provides valuable insights into how these models can be optimized and applied to real-world medical applications. Published: 2026-06-15T01:32:06+00:00 Venue: Neural Computing and Applications Score: 0.729 (consider)

Authors: Md. Fahim Hossen; Anirban Sarkar; Turja Majumder; Md. Fahmidun Nabi; Md. Faysal Ahamed; Fariya Bintay Shafi; Rusab Sarmun; Muhammad E. H. Chowdhury

Journal: Neural Computing and Applications

DOI: 10.1007/s00521-026-12198-6

Relevance: 0.729 (consider)

Abstract U-Net has become a fundamental method in medical image segmentation with its architecture evolving to tackle complex segmentation tasks across modalities such as Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), Computed Tomography (CT) scans and microscopic images. This review offers a comprehensive analysis of various U-Net architectures including U-Net++, Residual U-Net, Dense U-Net and more recent transformer-based models like TransUNet and Swin-UNet. These architectures introduce optimizations like improved feature propagation, gradient flow and self-attention mechanisms, significantly enhancing segmentation accuracy. Despite its widespread success, U-Net faces limitations in handling data imbalance, computational complexity and challenges with multi-modal and large-scale data. The integration of federated learning with U-Net addresses privacy concerns by enabling secure, collaborative model training across healthcare institutions while maintaining data confidentiality. This review also highlights the applications of U-Net variants in key medical imaging tasks, including brain segmentation, retinal vessel segmentation, cell and nuclei segmentation, prostate segmentation and skin lesion segmentation, which play a critical role in early disease detection and treatment planning. In addition to exploring these applications, the paper examines benchmark datasets, loss functions and evaluation metrics essential for assessing U-Net architectures. The review also discusses emerging trends like self-supervised learning, lightweight models for resource-constrained environments and interpretability techniques, offering potential directions for future research. By presenting both the advancements and limitations of U-Net, this review provides valuable insights into how these models can be optimized and applied to real-world medical applications.

item.title: MMARNet: Two-Stage Remote Sensing Image Registration with Multimodal Attention Mechanism
item.dc:creator: Xiangzeng Liu
Guanglu Shi
Zhipeng Huang
Jian Ji
Qiguang Miao
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: Multimodal image registration is a fundamental yet challenging task, particularly in remote sensing scenarios involving cross-platform, multi-temporal, and cross-modal data. The primary difficulty arises from the coexistence of large-scale geometric distortions and complex local appearance variations across modalities, which makes it difficult for a single-stage model to achieve both global alignment and fine-grained correspondence simultaneously. To address this issue, we propose MMARNet, a task-driven coarse-to-fine registration framework that explicitly decomposes multimodal registration into global geometric alignment and local correspondence refinement. Instead of treating registration as a unified problem, the proposed framework sequentially resolves distinct sources of error, leading to improved robustness and accuracy under challenging conditions. In the first stage, MMARNet learns geometry-aware global alignment by identifying structurally reliable regions across modalities and estimating large-scale transformations, effectively reducing the initial misalignment and normalizing the geometric space. In the second stage, the model focuses on residual local discrepancies by learning context-enhanced feature representations, enabling robust keypoint-level matching even under severe modality differences and nonlinear distortions. The two stages are designed to work in a complementary manner, where global alignment significantly simplifies the subsequent local matching process. Extensive experiments on three challenging multimodal datasets demonstrate that MMARNet achieves superior performance in both accuracy and robustness compared to existing methods. The results validate the effectiveness of the proposed problem decomposition and highlight the advantage of the coarse-to-fine optimization strategy for multimodal remote sensing image registration. Published: 2026-06-15T08:57:58+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.727 (consider)

Multimodal image registration is a fundamental yet challenging task, particularly in remote sensing scenarios involving cross-platform, multi-temporal, and cross-modal data. The primary difficulty arises from the coexistence of large-scale geometric distortions and complex local appearance variations across modalities, which makes it difficult for a single-stage model to achieve both global alignment and fine-grained correspondence simultaneously. To address this issue, we propose MMARNet, a task-driven coarse-to-fine registration framework that explicitly decomposes multimodal registration into global geometric alignment and local correspondence refinement. Instead of treating registration as a unified problem, the proposed framework sequentially resolves distinct sources of error, leading to improved robustness and accuracy under challenging conditions. In the first stage, MMARNet learns geometry-aware global alignment by identifying structurally reliable regions across modalities and estimating large-scale transformations, effectively reducing the initial misalignment and normalizing the geometric space. In the second stage, the model focuses on residual local discrepancies by learning context-enhanced feature representations, enabling robust keypoint-level matching even under severe modality differences and nonlinear distortions. The two stages are designed to work in a complementary manner, where global alignment significantly simplifies the subsequent local matching process. Extensive experiments on three challenging multimodal datasets demonstrate that MMARNet achieves superior performance in both accuracy and robustness compared to existing methods. The results validate the effectiveness of the proposed problem decomposition and highlight the advantage of the coarse-to-fine optimization strategy for multimodal remote sensing image registration.

item.title: Compensation of the Propagation and Clutter Effects of Rainfall for Pol-SAR-Based Sea-Surface Target Detection
item.dc:creator: Chenhao Wang
Xinjie Ju
Songyi Wang
Jianxiong Zhou
Jianbing Li
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (Pol-SAR) is one of the most important approaches for sea-surface target detection, but under rainfall conditions it tends to be distorted by the electromagnetic (EM) propagation effects and clutter interference of rainfall. To address this problem, this paper proposes a joint compensation method to mitigate the impacts of rainfall on the detection of sea-surface targets. In the method, a composite imaging model that thoroughly takes into account the propagation and scattering effects of rainfall, sea surface, and ship targets is first established. Then, a range-wise algorithm is proposed to effectively estimate the propagation effects, which are used to compensate for the radar echoes distorted by rainfall. Consequently, a hierarchical search strategy is employed to optimize the receiving polarization state to better discriminate the targets from rainfall and sea clutter. Simulation results show that, across the tested sea-surface wind and rainfall conditions, the proposed method improves the signal-to-clutter-plus-noise ratio (SCNR) by 4 to 13 dB compared with the polarimetric whitening filter, demonstrating its effectiveness under coupled rain–sea conditions. Published: 2026-06-15T03:01:27+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.727 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chenhao Wang; Xinjie Ju; Songyi Wang; Jianxiong Zhou; Jianbing Li

Journal: Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.3390/rs18121964

Relevance: 0.727 (consider)

Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (Pol-SAR) is one of the most important approaches for sea-surface target detection, but under rainfall conditions it tends to be distorted by the electromagnetic (EM) propagation effects and clutter interference of rainfall. To address this problem, this paper proposes a joint compensation method to mitigate the impacts of rainfall on the detection of sea-surface targets. In the method, a composite imaging model that thoroughly takes into account the propagation and scattering effects of rainfall, sea surface, and ship targets is first established. Then, a range-wise algorithm is proposed to effectively estimate the propagation effects, which are used to compensate for the radar echoes distorted by rainfall. Consequently, a hierarchical search strategy is employed to optimize the receiving polarization state to better discriminate the targets from rainfall and sea clutter. Simulation results show that, across the tested sea-surface wind and rainfall conditions, the proposed method improves the signal-to-clutter-plus-noise ratio (SCNR) by 4 to 13 dB compared with the polarimetric whitening filter, demonstrating its effectiveness under coupled rain–sea conditions.

item.title: Multimodal Prompt Learning for Spatial Reasoning in Remote Sensing Image Scene
item.dc:creator: Yan Ren
Haizhong Qian
Bingchuan Jiang
Tingting Li
Xiao Wang
Long Sun
Li Yang
item.prism:publicationName: Remote Sensing
item.description: A remote sensing scene graph (RSSG) enables machines to interpret interactions among ground objects in remote sensing images and supports semantic reasoning and description, thus making it a fundamental technique in the field. However, most existing scene reasoning approaches cannot fully utilize multimodal information, resulting in limited performance when inferring spatial relationships among ground objects. To this end, we propose a Unified Visual-Semantic Triple Prompt Learning (UVSTPL) framework, which integrates visual features with matched geospatial object labels, leverages a prompt learning module for multimodal feature extraction, and employs a refined UVTransE model to predict spatial relationships. The core principle of UVSTPL is to enhance semantic feature extraction and improve relationship prediction performance via the collaborative fusion of visual and linguistic modalities. To strengthen the model’s ability to reason about the spatial relationships among ground objects in images, a novel Geo-RSSG dataset is constructed, which includes precise annotations of geographic entities, spatial relationships, and attributes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed UVSTPL method outperforms benchmark models on the spatial relationship prediction task. In comparison with the best baseline method, our approach improves prediction precision by 1.85%, mean precision by 8.49%, mean recall by 17.46%, and mean F1-score by 12.97%. This study offers valuable insights for advancing the understanding and cognitive capabilities of remote sensing scenes. Published: 2026-06-15T03:01:27+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing Score: 0.727 (consider)

A remote sensing scene graph (RSSG) enables machines to interpret interactions among ground objects in remote sensing images and supports semantic reasoning and description, thus making it a fundamental technique in the field. However, most existing scene reasoning approaches cannot fully utilize multimodal information, resulting in limited performance when inferring spatial relationships among ground objects. To this end, we propose a Unified Visual-Semantic Triple Prompt Learning (UVSTPL) framework, which integrates visual features with matched geospatial object labels, leverages a prompt learning module for multimodal feature extraction, and employs a refined UVTransE model to predict spatial relationships. The core principle of UVSTPL is to enhance semantic feature extraction and improve relationship prediction performance via the collaborative fusion of visual and linguistic modalities. To strengthen the model’s ability to reason about the spatial relationships among ground objects in images, a novel Geo-RSSG dataset is constructed, which includes precise annotations of geographic entities, spatial relationships, and attributes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed UVSTPL method outperforms benchmark models on the spatial relationship prediction task. In comparison with the best baseline method, our approach improves prediction precision by 1.85%, mean precision by 8.49%, mean recall by 17.46%, and mean F1-score by 12.97%. This study offers valuable insights for advancing the understanding and cognitive capabilities of remote sensing scenes.

item.title: Scaling Rough Terrain Locomotion With Automatic Curriculum Reinforcement Learning
item.dc:creator: Ziming Li
Chenhao Li
Marco Hutter
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
item.description: Curriculum learning has demonstrated substantial effectiveness in robot learning. However, it still faces limitations when scaling to complex, wide-ranging task spaces. Such task spaces often lack a well-defined difficulty structure, making the difficulty ordering required by previous methods challenging to define. We propose a Learning Progress-based Automatic Curriculum Reinforcement Learning (LP-ACRL) framework, which estimates the agent's learning progress online and adaptively adjusts the task-sampling distribution, thereby enabling automatic curriculum generation without prior knowledge of the difficulty distribution over the task space. Policies trained with LP-ACRL enable the ANYmal D quadruped to achieve and maintain stable, high-speed locomotion at 2.5 m/s linear velocity and 3.0,/s angular velocity across diverse terrains, including stairs, slopes, gravel, and low-friction flat surfaces–whereas previous methods have generally been limited to high speeds on flat terrain or low speeds on complex terrain. Experimental results demonstrate that LP-ACRL exhibits strong scalability and real-world applicability, providing a robust baseline for future research on curriculum generation in complex, wide-ranging robotic learning task spaces. Published: 2026-06-15T19:49:11+00:00 Venue: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters Score: 0.726 (consider)

Relevance: 0.726 (consider)

Curriculum learning has demonstrated substantial effectiveness in robot learning. However, it still faces limitations when scaling to complex, wide-ranging task spaces. Such task spaces often lack a well-defined difficulty structure, making the difficulty ordering required by previous methods challenging to define. We propose a Learning Progress-based Automatic Curriculum Reinforcement Learning (LP-ACRL) framework, which estimates the agent's learning progress online and adaptively adjusts the task-sampling distribution, thereby enabling automatic curriculum generation without prior knowledge of the difficulty distribution over the task space. Policies trained with LP-ACRL enable the ANYmal D quadruped to achieve and maintain stable, high-speed locomotion at 2.5 m/s linear velocity and 3.0,/s angular velocity across diverse terrains, including stairs, slopes, gravel, and low-friction flat surfaces–whereas previous methods have generally been limited to high speeds on flat terrain or low speeds on complex terrain. Experimental results demonstrate that LP-ACRL exhibits strong scalability and real-world applicability, providing a robust baseline for future research on curriculum generation in complex, wide-ranging robotic learning task spaces.
